cards.front: Can you pass structs to functions?
cards.back: Yes. Structs can be passed by value, reference, or const reference, just like fundamental types.

cards.text: For read-only struct parameters, prefer passing by {{c1::const reference}} to avoid {{c2::copying}}.

cards.question: What parameter passing method is used?

cards.back: Yes. Functions can return structs by value, allowing you to return multiple related values together.

cards.question: What does this function return?
cards.answer: Returns a Point struct initialized with the given x and y values.
cards.explanation: Aggregate initialization in return statement creates struct.

cards.text: To modify a struct parameter, pass by {{c1::non-const reference}}: void update(Employee{{c2::&}} e)

cards.front: When should you pass structs by value vs by reference?
cards.back:
• By value: Small structs (2-3 scalars), want a copy
  • By const ref: Large structs, read-only access
  • By ref: Need to modify the struct

cards.question: What's the benefit of this return style?
cards.answer: Returns multiple related values in a single struct without needing output parameters.
cards.explanation: Cleaner than multiple output parameters or std::pair/tuple.

cards.front: What happens when you return a struct by value from a function?
cards.back: The struct is typically returned via copy elision (RVO), avoiding unnecessary copies. Modern C++ optimizes this efficiently.

cards.concept: Using structs for multiple return values
cards.explanation:
Structs provide clean multiple return values:
  • More readable than std::pair/tuple
  • Self-documenting with named members
  • Type-safe and compiler-checked
  • Efficient with RVO
Better than output parameters.
examples: struct Result { bool success; int value; };
auto [ok, val] = compute();  // C++17 structured binding

cards.front: Why prefer structs over std::pair for multiple return values?
cards.back: Structs have named members (self-documenting) while std::pair uses generic first/second, making code less clear.

cards.text: For large read-only structs, pass by {{c1::const reference}} to avoid {{c2::copying overhead}}.
To modify a struct parameter, pass by {{c1::non-const reference}}: void update(Employee{{c2::&}} e)
Functions can return structs by {{c1::value}}, efficiently using {{c2::RVO}} (Return Value Optimization).
For large read-only structs, pass by {{c1::const reference}} to avoid {{c2::copying overhead}}.
To modify a struct parameter, pass by {{c1::non-const reference}}: void update(Employee{{c2::&}} e)
